result:
I’ve been thinking a lot about silence lately. Not the absence of sound, but the kind that exists underneath the noise—the deep, tectonic silence that holds everything up. We spend our lives running from it, don’t we? Plugging our ears with podcasts, our eyes with screens, our hearts with the constant, low-grade hum of being busy. We mistake chatter for communication and activity for purpose. But it’s in the silence that the most interesting things grow, like mushrooms in the dark, damp hollows of a forest floor.

My grandmother’s house was full of this kind of silence. It wasn’t empty or lonely; it was fertile. You could walk in, and the quiet would settle around your shoulders like a shawl. She’d be in her kitchen, kneading dough without a radio on. The only sounds were the soft thump-thump of her palms against the flour-dusted counter, the creak of the old floorboard near the icebox, the distant, syncopated drip of the kitchen tap. In that silence, you could hear your own thoughts unspool. You could hear the house itself—the sigh of settling timbers, the whisper of dust motes drifting in a sunbeam. That silence wasn’t passive; it was an active presence. It was the space where the taste of the coming bread was already imagined, where memories drifted up like steam from a cup of tea, where a whole lifetime of loves and losses was held, not spoken, but deeply known.

We’ve traded that for a frantic, skimming quality of life. Our silence is now merely a buffer between notifications. We have forgotten how to be bored, and in doing so, we have forgotten how to dream. Boredom is the antechamber of creativity. It is the itchy, restless feeling that precedes a poem, a sketch on a napkin, a sudden decision to reorganize the bookshelf by colour. When we kill boredom the moment it twitches—reaching for the phone, queuing up another episode—we abort that process. We never reach the quiet, desperate point where the mind, left with nothing external to consume, turns inward and begins to generate its own world.

I remember, as a child, long car journeys before portable DVDs and tablets. You’d stare out the window at the hypnotic blur of fences and fields, and eventually, your brain would get so tired of the monotony that it would start putting on a show. That cloud became a dragon, that barn became a castle, your own breath on the glass became a canvas for your finger. You constructed entire sagas from the raw, dull material of nothing. You were forced into an intimacy with your own imagination. That is a sacred space. I worry we are boarding up the entrance to that space, one click at a time.

And in the wider world, we’ve lost the silence between people. Conversation now feels like a competitive sport of anecdotes and hot takes, a frantic volley where the pauses are seen as weaknesses to be filled. But what about the silence shared between two people who know each other well? It’s the silence that sits comfortably during a long drive. It’s the silence of working side-by-side in a garden, pulling weeds, where the companionship is in the shared activity, not the dialogue. It’s the heavy, profound silence of sitting with someone in grief, where words are not just inadequate, but insulting—where the only true thing to offer is the quiet witness of your presence. That kind of silence is a language unto itself. It says, “I am here. You are not alone in your experience. We can hold this, together, without dressing it up in syllables.”

Perhaps our fear of silence is fundamentally a fear of ourselves. In the quiet, we meet the unvarnished truth of our own minds—the regrets that ping like sonar, the anxieties that swirl, the unresolved melodies of old joys. It can be unsettling. It’s easier to be a noun defined by external things—a job, a relationship, a political stance, a消费 history—than to be the verb that is constantly becoming in the quiet dark. But it is only there, in that unsupervised inner space, that we can do the essential work of integration. We stitch together the fragments of our days into a narrative that makes sense. We digest our experiences. We forgive ourselves.

So, I am making little raids on the kingdom of noise. I’ve started taking walks without headphones. At first, it was agonizing. My brain, like a caged animal, rattled its bars with to-do lists and song lyrics and fragments of worry. But slowly, the internal cacophony began to subside. I began to hear the layered text of the world: the wind combing through different types of leaves (raspy for oak, whispery for birch), the fractal chatter of birdsong, the rhythmic crunch of my own footsteps acting as a metronome. The thinking part of my mind got tired and eventually quiet, and a more ancient, observing part woke up.

I’ll leave the dishes in the sink for ten minutes and just stare out the window at the way the evening light gilds the bricks of the building across the street. I’ll sit on the floor with my back against the couch and do absolutely nothing. The goal is not productivity or even “mindfulness” as a checked box. The goal is to reclaim a little sovereignty over my own attention. To court the boredom. To listen for the silence underneath.

Because in that silence, something else speaks. Call it intuition, call it the subconscious, call it the soul or simply a deeper level of neural processing. It’s the part that solves the problem you’ve been hammering at while you’re taking a shower. It’s the part that delivers the perfect sentence as you’re falling asleep. It’s the wellspring of empathy that allows you to truly feel another’s pain. It doesn’t shout over the din. It waits. It whispers.

We are so busy broadcasting our lives—curating, posting, sharing, commenting—that we have cut ourselves off from the receiving frequency. We are all transmission and no reception. Silence is the reset. It is the white space around the text that makes the words legible. It is the pause between the musical notes that makes the melody. It is the fertile, dark void from which every single thing that matters must eventually emerge.

So here’s to the quiet. Here’s to the unplugged, unproductive, un-optimized moments. Here’s to staring into space. Here’s to listening to the house breathe. Here’s to the courage to sit with oneself, in all one’s glorious and messy contradiction, and just be. The world is screaming. The most radical act may be to lower your voice, close your eyes, and turn down the volume on everything, until all you can hear is your own heart, beating its steady, patient rhythm in the dark. That’s where everything begins.
